title: Making a Strong First and Last Impression: Introductions and Conclusions
simple_explanation: Imagine your argument is a present. The introduction is the shiny wrapping paper, making people excited to open it! The conclusion is the big, fancy bow that ties it all up neatly. This page teaches you how to make your wrapping paper super exciting and your bow perfectly tied, so your argument looks amazing from start to finish.
detailed_explanation: A good introduction for a 'balanced argument' should gently set the scene and introduce the topic in a fair way, without immediately picking a side. It might even use clever 'rhetorical questions' to make the reader think. Rhetorical questions are special questions that don't need an answer; their job is to make people pause and consider your topic deeply, drawing them into your argument. Your introduction should also tell the reader what to expect, letting them know that your argument will explore different points of view on the topic. A powerful conclusion brings everything together. It sums up both sides of the argument you've discussed and leaves the reader with a clear final thought, making your argument feel complete and memorable.

examples_expanded.text: Introduction example: 'Anyone who has seen the rubbish littering our classrooms must want a solution. However, plans to make the oldest children clear it up are considered unfair. Who is right? This argument will explore both opinions.'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of introductions and conclusions for arguments in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Rhetorical question: 'Who wouldn't want to be a millionaire?' (It makes you think, but you don't actually answer aloud.)
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of introductions and conclusions for arguments in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Conclusion example: 'In conclusion, while litter is unsightly and a danger, it is the responsibility of all of us who use the school space.'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of introductions and conclusions for arguments in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'balanced argument' in a sentence. Remember it means: an argument that presents more than one point of view, discussing both 'for' and 'against' sides.
Match the word 'rhetorical questions' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Introductions and conclusions for arguments.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'balanced argument' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Introductions and conclusions frame your argument, making it impactful.
A good introduction sets the scene and introduces the topic fairly.
Rhetorical questions engage the reader and make them think.
A conclusion summarizes both sides of a balanced argument and provides a final thought.
Clear beginnings and endings help your argument be understood and remembered.

vocabulary.definition: an argument that presents more than one point of view, discussing both 'for' and 'against' sides.
questions asked to make a point or create a thought, rather than to get a direct answer.
the opening part of an argument that sets the scene for the topic.
the final part of an argument that summarizes the main points and gives a closing statement.
